children.title: Preface
children.summary:
Here is a dense 5-sentence routing summary of the "Preface" section:

This textbook provides an undergraduate course on the Theory of Computation, covering topics such as computability theory, automata theory, and complexity theory. The course aims to develop formal mathematical models of computation that reflect real-world computers, with a focus on understanding the limitations of computers. The book is based on a 12-week term with three hours of classes per week, covering most of the material from Chapters 2-5. The course has been influenced by several textbooks, including "Introduction to the Theory of Computation" and "Einf¨uhrung in die Theoretische Informatik". The book is intended for students who want to learn about the Theory of Computation, with a focus on developing formal mathematical models of computation and understanding the limitations of computers.

children.title: Purpose and motivation

This chapter explores the fundamental nature of computer science through the lens of complexity theory, computability theory, and automata theory. These three core areas of study aim to understand the limits of computational power, classifying problems as solvable or unsolvable, and examining the degree of difficulty in solving computational problems. By delving into these theoretical foundations, students will gain a deeper understanding of computers as abstract mathematical models, with applications in programming languages, compilers, artificial intelligence, computer security, and more. The ultimate goal is to bridge theory and practice, influencing subsequent courses like Compilers and Artificial Intelligence, while developing problem-solving skills and analytical thinking. children.title: Complexity theory
children.summary:
Here is a dense 5-sentence routing summary for the section on "Complexity Theory":

"Complexity theory explores the distinction between computationally hard and easy problems, with a focus on classifying problems by their degree of difficulty. Informally, 'easy' problems are efficiently solvable, while 'hard' problems are either unsolvable or require unknown efficient solutions. The central question in complexity theory is to provide rigorous proofs that problems deemed 'hard' remain so. Computability theory, a related field, investigates the solvability and unsolvability of mathematical statements, requiring formal definitions of computer, algorithm, and computation. By classifying problems as solvable or unsolvable, both fields aim to understand the limits of computational power."

children.title: Computability theory
children.summary:
Here is a dense, 5-sentence routing summary of the section on Computability theory:

Computability theory explores the fundamental capabilities and limitations of computers, focusing on classifying problems as solvable or unsolvable. This area builds upon complexity theory, examining the degree of difficulty in solving computational problems, with central questions including the classification of problems and rigorous proofs of hardness. Theoretical models such as Turing machines are used to understand solvable and unsolvable problems, with applications in computer science, artificial intelligence, and programming languages. Automata theory is also covered, examining definitions and properties of different computation models, including finite automata, context-free grammars, and Turing machines. This course provides a theoretical foundation for understanding the capabilities and limitations of computers, with relevance to practice in areas such as compiler design, computer security, and artificial intelligence.

children.title: Automata theory
children.summary:
Here is a dense, 5-sentence routing summary of the section on Automata theory:

Automata theory explores the definitions and properties of various computation models, including Finite Automata, Context-Free Grammars, and Turing Machines. The core question in this field is whether these models have equivalent computational power or if one can solve more problems than others. This course will delve into Automata Theory, examining its relevance to computer science, mathematical proof techniques, and practical applications such as programming language design, compiler development, and artificial intelligence. By studying Automata Theory, students will gain insight into the fundamental capabilities and limitations of computers, as well as develop problem-solving skills through theoretical analysis and proof-based reasoning. This course is essential for understanding the underlying principles of computer science and its relevance to real-world applications in fields like software engineering, security, and data processing.

children.title: Proof techniques
children.summary:
Here is a cohesive 5-sentence summary for the parent chapter "Proof Techniques":

This chapter provides an overview of various proof techniques used in mathematics, including direct proofs, constructive and nonconstructive proofs, proofs by contradiction, the Pigeon Hole Principle, and proofs by induction. Direct proofs involve breaking down statements into smaller components to establish their truth, while constructive and nonconstructive proofs demonstrate existence through creation or logical reasoning, respectively. Proofs by contradiction assume a statement's falsity to derive a logical contradiction, proving its truth, and proofs by induction prove statements true for all positive integers through basis and induction steps. The chapter also explores the Pigeon Hole Principle, which has applications in various areas of mathematics, including proofs by induction, and provides examples of proof techniques used in graph theory, such as Euler's theorem. By mastering these proof techniques, readers can develop a deeper understanding of mathematical concepts and principles, ultimately leading to a more comprehensive comprehension of the subject matter.

children.title: Direct proofs
children.summary:
Here is a dense, 5-sentence routing summary of the section on "Direct proofs":

Direct proofs involve approaching a theorem directly without relying on auxiliary statements or lemmas. This technique requires understanding the statement to be proved and working out simple cases to gain insight. The proof involves breaking down the statement into smaller components and using logical reasoning to establish the truth of each component. In direct proofs, the focus is on providing a clear and precise argument that demonstrates the theorem's validity. By following this approach, one can develop a thorough understanding of the underlying concepts and principles, ultimately leading to a deeper comprehension of the subject matter.

children.title: Constructive proofs
children.summary:
Here is a dense, 5-sentence routing summary for the section on "Constructive proofs":

Constructive proofs demonstrate the existence of an object by providing a method to create it, whereas nonconstructive proofs only show existence without creation. Theorem 1.3.4 illustrates this concept with the example of a 3-regular graph having n vertices for every even integer n ≥4. Constructive proof techniques involve defining specific objects and showing they satisfy desired properties, as seen in Theorem 1.3.5. In contrast, nonconstructive proofs, such as Theorem 1.3.6, only establish existence without providing examples or methods of creation. By highlighting the distinction between constructive and nonconstructive proof techniques, this section provides a comprehensive overview of proof strategies in graph theory.

children.title: Nonconstructive proofs
children.summary:
Here is a dense, 5-sentence routing summary for the section on "Nonconstructive proofs":

Nonconstructive proofs establish the existence of an object without explicitly creating it, often using proof techniques such as cases, contradictions, and assumptions. Examples include Theorems 1.3.6 and 1.3.9, which demonstrate the existence of irrational numbers with specific properties through logical reasoning. Proof by contradiction is a common method used in nonconstructive proofs, where an assumption leads to a contradiction, thereby proving the original statement. This technique is employed in Theorems 1.3.8 and 1.3.9, which use contradictions to establish the irrationality of √2. Nonconstructive proofs rely on logical reasoning and mathematical principles to demonstrate the existence of objects without requiring their explicit construction.

children.title: Proofs by contradiction
children.summary:
Here is a dense, 5-sentence routing summary of the section on "Proofs by Contradiction":

"Proofs by contradiction" is a proof technique where a statement's falsity is assumed to derive a logical contradiction, thereby proving its truth. This method involves assuming the negation of the statement to be proven and then showing that this assumption leads to an impossible or absurd conclusion. The contrapositive of the original statement is logically equivalent to the original statement, making it sufficient to prove the original statement's truth. Proofs by contradiction are used in various mathematical theorems, including those on even numbers, irrational numbers, and sequences. By assuming a statement's falsity and deriving a logical contradiction, one can conclude that the original statement must be true, providing a rigorous proof of its validity.

children.title: The pigeon hole principle
children.summary:
Here is a dense, 5-sentence routing summary of the section:

The Pigeon Hole Principle: If n+1 or more objects are placed into n boxes, at least one box contains two or more objects. The principle implies that there cannot be a one-to-one function from a set with m elements to a set with n elements if m > n. A theorem states that every sequence of n^2 + 1 distinct real numbers contains an increasing or decreasing subsequence of length n+1. This proof uses the Pigeon Hole Principle and is based on contradiction, assuming no such subsequence exists and then showing this leads to a contradiction. The principle has applications in various areas, including proofs by induction, which involves proving a statement true for all positive integers through basis and induction steps.

children.title: Proofs by induction
children.summary:
Here is a dense, 5-sentence routing summary of the section on "Proofs by Induction":

"Proofs by induction: a powerful technique for proving mathematical statements P(n) true for all positive integers n, involving basis (P(1) proven), induction step (proving P(n+1) true if P(n) is true), and application to specific theorems such as sum of arithmetic series and divisibility of polynomials."

children.title: Finite Automata and Regular Languages

This chapter introduces deterministic finite automata (DFAs) as mathematical models to recognize and accept regular languages. DFAs are defined by their set of states, alphabet, transition function, start state, and accept states, allowing them to recognize languages with specific substrings or patterns. The chapter explores regular language properties, including union, concatenation, and star operations, which enable the construction of new regular languages from existing ones. Regular expressions are also introduced as a means to describe languages, with their class coinciding with the class of regular languages. By understanding DFAs, regular expressions, and their relationships, readers can gain insight into the fundamental concepts of formal language theory and develop skills in designing and analyzing finite automata models.

children.title: Deterministic finite automata
children.summary:
Here is a cohesive 5-sentence summary for the parent chapter "Deterministic finite automata":

This chapter introduces deterministic finite automata (DFAs), which are mathematical models used to recognize and accept regular languages. A DFA consists of a set of states, an alphabet, a transition function, a start state, and a set of accept states, and is defined as M = (Q, Σ, δ, q, F) where Q represents the set of states, Σ is the input alphabet, δ is the transition function, q is the start state, and F is the set of accept states. DFAs can be used to recognize languages that contain specific substrings or patterns, such as binary strings with "101" as a substring, and demonstrate the formal definition of a DFA, including its components and operations. The chapter also explores regular language properties, including union, concatenation, and star operations, which allow for the construction of new regular languages from existing ones. By understanding DFAs and their applications, readers can gain insight into the fundamental concepts of formal language theory and develop skills in designing and analyzing finite automata models.

**Finite Automata Routing Summary**

children.title: Regular operations
children.summary:
Here is a dense, 5-sentence routing summary of the section 'Regular operations':

The regular operations on languages include union (∪), concatenation (AB), and star operation (*). The union of two languages A and B is defined as {w : w ∈A or w ∈B}, while the concatenation AB is defined as {ww′ : w ∈A and w′ ∈B}. The star operation * is defined as {u1u2 ... uk : k ≥0 and ui ∈A for all i = 1, 2, ..., k}. The set of regular languages is closed under these operations, including union, concatenation, and star. This can be proven using finite automata, with the closure under union being demonstrated through the construction of a new finite automaton that accepts the union of two regular languages.

children.title: Nondeterministic finite automata
children.summary:
Here is a cohesive 5-sentence summary for the parent chapter 'Nondeterministic finite automata':

A nondeterministic finite automaton (NFA) is a type of automaton that exhibits distinct behavior compared to its deterministic counterparts, characterized by non-deterministic transitions and epsilon transitions. NFAs can accept strings with at least one path from the start state to an accept state, without hanging on the entire input, and use a guessing mechanism to verify substring presence. The definition of an NFA is a 5-tuple M = (Q, Σ, δ, q, F), where Q is a finite set of states, Σ is an alphabet, δ is a transition function mapping states and symbols to subsets of states, q is the start state, and F is a subset of accept states. NFAs can be used to recognize regular languages through nondeterministic transitions, accepting binary strings containing specific substrings and rejecting others. The behavior of an NFA can be converted to a Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA) using a formal conversion process, resulting in the same language acceptance with L(M) = L(N).

children.title: A first example
children.summary:
Here is a dense, 5-sentence routing summary of the section "A first example":

Nondeterministic finite automata (NFAs) exhibit distinct behavior compared to their deterministic counterparts. The NFA presented in this example demonstrates three key differences: non-deterministic transitions, epsilon transitions, and inability to continue in certain states. Upon processing the input string 010110, the NFA yields seven possible computations, with exactly two ending in the accept state q4. Conversely, the same NFA rejects the input string 010 due to a computation that hangs indefinitely. The NFA's behavior is characterized by its acceptance of binary strings containing specific substrings and rejection of others, highlighting its ability to recognize regular languages through nondeterministic transitions.

children.title: Definition of nondeterministic finite automaton
children.summary:
Here is a dense, 5-sentence routing summary of the section on 'Definition of nondeterministic finite automaton':

Nondeterministic Finite Automata (NFAs) are defined as 5-tuples M = (Q, Σ, δ, q, F), where Q is a finite set of states, Σ is an alphabet, δ is a transition function mapping states and symbols to subsets of states, q is the start state, and F is a subset of accept states. NFAs make decisions by "guessing" whether to check for membership in A1 or A2 based on input string length, then verifying the guess through transitions. The language accepted by an NFA L(M) is defined as {w ∈Σ∗: M accepts w}, and can be converted to a Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA) using a formal conversion process. This conversion involves mapping the DFA's transition function δ to an NFA's transition function δ′, which maps states and symbols to subsets of states. The resulting NFA has the same behavior as the original DFA, with L(M) = L(N).

children.title: Equivalence of DFAs and NFAs
children.summary:
Here is a cohesive 5-sentence summary:

The equivalence between deterministic finite automata (DFAs) and nondeterministic finite automata (NFAs) can be demonstrated through the conversion of regular languages from NFAs to DFAs, preserving the same accepted language. This conversion involves defining new states and transition functions based on the NFA's original states and transitions. The resulting DFA has a start state defined as all reachable states from the NFA's start state after zero or more ε-transitions, and accept states are those containing the accept state of the NFA. This process showcases that regular languages are closed under the union operation, as shown in Theorem 2.6.1. By converting NFAs to DFAs, we can equivalently represent the same language, highlighting the relationship between these two types of automata.

children.title: An example
children.summary:
Here is a dense, 5-sentence routing summary of this section:

Regular languages can be converted from nondeterministic finite automata (NFAs) to deterministic finite automata (DFAs), preserving the same language accepted by both. The conversion involves defining new states and transition functions based on the NFA's original states and transitions. Specifically, the start state of the DFA is defined as the set of all reachable states from the NFA's start state after zero or more ε-transitions. The accept states of the DFA are those containing the accept state of the NFA. This conversion demonstrates that regular languages are closed under the union operation, as shown in Theorem 2.6.1.

children.title: Closure under the regular operations
children.summary:
Here is a dense, 5-sentence routing summary for the section "Closure under the regular operations":

Regular languages are closed under union, concatenation, and star operations, as demonstrated by Theorems 2.6.1, 2.6.2, and 2.6.3, respectively. These theorems utilize NFA constructions to prove that the resulting language is also regular, leveraging properties such as state splitting and epsilon transitions. Closure under union allows for the combination of two regular languages into a new regular language, while closure under concatenation enables the sequential composition of two regular languages. Closure under star operation ensures that the Kleene star of a regular language remains regular, providing a way to express non-terminating strings in regular expressions. These results establish a foundation for further properties and operations on regular languages, including complementation and intersection.

children.title: Regular expressions
children.summary:
Here is a dense, 5-sentence routing summary of the section on Regular Expressions:

Regular expressions are a means to describe languages, and their class coincides with the class of regular languages. A regular expression can be built from "building blocks" (individual characters or empty strings) using rules such as union (∪), concatenation (*), and Kleene star (∗). The language described by a regular expression can be determined using various identities involving regular expressions, which allow for algebraic manipulation of the expressions. Every regular language can be described by a regular expression, and conversely, every regular expression describes a regular language. The equivalence between regular expressions and regular languages is established through two proofs: one showing that every regular expression describes a regular language, and another demonstrating that every DFA can be converted to a regular expression describing its corresponding language.

children.title: Equivalence of regular expressions and regular languages
children.summary:
Here is a cohesive 5-sentence summary of the parent chapter "Equivalence of regular expressions and regular languages":

Regular expressions are equivalent to regular languages, as demonstrated through mathematical induction on the structure of the expression. The equivalence holds due to the preservation of regularity under union (∪) and concatenation (*), allowing for the conversion between regular expressions and finite automata (DFAs). Converting a DFA to a regular expression involves solving recurrence relations involving languages, leveraging techniques such as concatenation and union to express unknown languages in terms of known ones. This process relies on mathematical lemmas, specifically Lemma 2.8.2, which ensures that the system has a solution by guaranteeing that the empty string is not part of any equation. By applying this method to any given DFA, a regular expression can be obtained that describes its corresponding language, thereby establishing the equivalence between regular expressions and regular languages.

children.title: Every regular expression describes a regular language
children.summary:
Here is a dense 5-sentence routing summary of the section:

Regular expressions describe regular languages, as proven by induction on the structure of the expression. The base cases show that single characters and empty strings describe regular languages, while the inductive step demonstrates that union (∪) and concatenation (*) preserve regularity. Regular expressions can be converted to finite automata (DFAs), which accept the corresponding language. To convert a DFA to a regular expression, solve recurrence relations involving languages, considering cases where the string is in the known language C or the unknown language L. The goal is to express the unknown language L in terms of the known language B and C, using techniques such as concatenation and union.

children.title: Converting a DFA to a regular expression
children.summary:
Here is a dense, 5-sentence routing summary of the section "Converting a DFA to a regular expression":

The conversion from a deterministic finite automaton (DFA) to a regular expression involves solving recurrence relations involving languages, specifically using Lemma 2.8.2. The lemma states that if we have a set of equations in the form L = n[ j=1 BijLj ! ∪Ci for 1 ≤i ≤n, then L can be expressed as a regular expression only involving the regular expressions Bij and Ci. This process is repeated until all languages are expressed in terms of regular expressions, resulting in a single regular expression that describes the language accepted by the DFA. The conversion relies on the fact that ϵ ̸∈Bij for each equation, ensuring that the system has a solution. By applying this method to any given DFA, we can obtain a regular expression that describes its corresponding language.

children.title: The pumping lemma and nonregular languages
children.summary:
Here is a 5-sentence summary of the parent chapter "The pumping lemma and nonregular languages":

The pumping lemma is a fundamental tool used to demonstrate that certain languages are not regular. By applying the pumping lemma, it has been proven that specific languages such as {0n1n : n ≥0}, {w ∈{0, 1}∗: the number of occurrences of 01 in w equals the number of occurrences of 10 in w}, and {1n2 : n ≥0} are not regular. Additionally, the pumping lemma has been used to show that the language L = {w ∈{0, 1}∗: w is the binary representation of a prime number} is also not regular. In contrast, Higman's Theorem establishes that for any finite alphabet and language, the language consisting of subsequences of all strings in the original language is indeed regular. Overall, the pumping lemma plays a crucial role in proving the non-regularity of various languages, shedding light on the limitations of regular expression-based models.

children.title: Applications of the pumping lemma
children.summary:
Here is a dense 5-sentence routing summary of the section "Applications of the pumping lemma":

The pumping lemma is applied to demonstrate that certain languages are not regular. Specifically, the pumping lemma is used to prove that the languages {0n1n : n ≥0}, {w ∈{0, 1}∗: the number of occurrences of 01 in w equals the number of occurrences of 10 in w}, and {1n2 : n ≥0} are not regular. The pumping lemma is also used to prove that the language L = {w ∈{0, 1}∗: w is the binary representation of a prime number} is not regular. Additionally, Higman's Theorem states that for any finite alphabet Σ and any language L ⊆Σ∗, the language SUBSEQ(L) consisting of subsequences of all strings in L is regular.

children.title: Higman's Theorem
children.summary:
Here is a cohesive 5-sentence summary of Higman's Theorem:

Higman's Theorem establishes that the language SUBSEQ(L), consisting of subsequences of all strings in a non-empty language L, is regular if and only if L itself is regular. To prove this, the theorem relies on induction on the dimension n, assuming the result holds for subsets of Nn-1. By constructing a finite set A of binary strings with at most n many (0, 1)-alternations that contain all subsequences of L, the theorem shows that the set M of minimal elements in any subset S of Nn is finite. This finiteness of M then implies that SUBSEQ(L) is regular, as it can be described by a regular expression. Ultimately, Higman's Theorem provides a powerful tool for determining the regularity of languages based on their subsequence properties.

children.title: Dickson's Theorem
children.summary:
Here is a dense, 5-sentence routing summary of Dickson's Theorem:

Dickson's Theorem states that for any finite alphabet Σ and language L ⊆Σ∗, the set M of minimal elements in S (where S is a subset of Nn) is finite. This theorem is used to prove Higman's Theorem, which asserts that the language SUBSEQ(L), consisting of subsequences of all strings in L, is regular. Dickson's Theorem is proven by induction on the dimension n, with base case n = 1 and inductive step showing that M is finite for any subset S of Nn. The theorem relies on a key lemma stating that Mik, the set of minimal elements in Sik (a subset of Nn-1), is a subset of the set of all minimal elements in Sik. By combining this with the induction hypothesis, Dickson's Theorem establishes that M is finite for any subset S of Nn.

children.title: Proof of Higman's Theorem
children.summary:
Here is a dense, 5-sentence routing summary for the section "Proof of Higman's Theorem":

Higman's Theorem states that the set of all subsequences of a non-empty language L is regular if and only if L is regular. To prove this, we use induction on the dimension n, assuming the theorem holds for all subsets of Nn-1. We then consider a subset S of Nn and show that M, the set of minimal elements in S, is finite by constructing a finite set A of binary strings with at most n many (0, 1)-alternations such that SUBSEQ(L) ⊆ A ∩SUBSEQ(L). We also show that the language A is regular using a regular expression description. By combining these results, we prove that M is finite and thus SUBSEQ(L) is regular.

Keywords: Higman's Theorem, regular languages, subsequences, induction, minimal elements.

children.title: Context-Free Languages
children.summary:
Here is a cohesive 5-sentence summary for the parent chapter 'Context-Free Languages':

Context-free languages are defined using context-free grammars (CFGs), which consist of a finite set of variables, terminals, and rules that define the structure of strings in the language. Context-free languages can be equivalently represented by nondeterministic pushdown automata (PDAs) and context-free grammars, with each providing a unique way to analyze and process these languages. The pumping lemma for context-free languages states that any context-free language must have a finite pumping length p, such that any string in the language with length greater than or equal to p can be divided into three substrings where |vy| ≥ 1 and |vxy| ≤ p. Context-free grammars are used to generate languages that can be represented by properly nested parentheses, non-regular languages, and even regular languages, providing a powerful tool for studying the properties of formal language theory. By exploring these concepts, we gain a deeper understanding of the relationships between context-free languages, pushdown automata, and other formal systems, ultimately advancing our knowledge of the syntax and semantics of programming languages and their compilation.

children.title: Context-free grammars
children.summary:
Here is a dense 5-sentence routing summary of this section on context-free grammars:

Context-free languages are defined using context-free grammars (CFGs), which consist of a finite set of variables, terminals, and rules that define the structure of strings in the language. A CFG is a 4-tuple G = (V, Σ, R, S) where V is the set of variables, Σ is the set of terminals, R is the set of rules, and S is the start variable. The language of a CFG is defined as the set of all strings that can be derived from the start variable S using the rules in R, with derivation denoted by ⇒. Context-free grammars are equivalent to nondeterministic pushdown automata (NPDA) in terms of their power and complexity, and are used to define the syntax of programming languages and their compilation. Examples of context-free grammars include those that generate regular languages, nonregular languages such as {0n1n : n ≥0}, and languages with proper nested parentheses.

Here is a cohesive 5-sentence summary for the parent chapter "Examples of Context-Free Grammars":

Context-free grammars are used to generate languages that can be represented by properly nested parentheses, non-regular languages, and even regular languages. A key example is the language of properly nested parentheses, which can be generated by a context-free grammar with specific rules to ensure proper nesting. Additionally, context-free grammars can be constructed to prove that certain languages are not regular, such as the language {0n1n : n ≥ 0}. Furthermore, context-free grammars can also be used to demonstrate that regular languages are a subset of context-free languages by constructing a corresponding grammar for each regular language. By exploring these examples, we can gain a deeper understanding of the properties and applications of context-free grammars in formal language theory.

children.title: Properly nested parentheses
children.summary:
Here is a dense, 5-sentence routing summary of the section on "Properly nested parentheses":

Context-free grammar G = (V, Σ, R, S) generates the language L(G) consisting of all strings of properly nested parentheses, where V = {S}, Σ = {a, b}, and R = {S →ϵ, S →aSb, S →SS}. The language is defined as the set of all strings derivable from the start variable S using zero or more applications of the rules in R. Properly nested parentheses can be represented by: (1) an empty string (∅), (2) a left-parenthesis followed by an arbitrary string of properly nested parentheses and a right-parenthesis, or (3) two arbitrary strings of properly nested parentheses. The grammar G is used to demonstrate that the language of properly nested parentheses is context-free, as it can be generated by a context-free grammar. This section also provides an example of a context-free grammar for a non-regular language, L1 = {0n1n : n ≥0}.

children.title: Regular languages are context-free
children.summary:
Here is a cohesive 5-sentence summary:

Regular languages are indeed context-free, as demonstrated by the conversion of deterministic finite automata (DFAs) to context-free grammars. This process involves transforming DFAs into Chomsky normal form, which preserves the original language while eliminating unnecessary complexity. By applying this transformation, any context-free grammar can be converted to Chomsky normal form, providing a standardized representation of context-free languages. The resulting Chomsky normal form grammars have rules that adhere to specific structural constraints, ensuring consistency and clarity in their expression. This equivalence between regular and context-free languages has significant implications for the study and application of formal language theory.

This transformation involves eliminating the start variable from the right-hand side of rules and ensuring that the language remains unchanged. The resulting grammar G has rules of the form A →BC, A →a, or S →ϵ, where A is a variable and w is a string over the alphabet V ∪Σ. By applying this transformation to any context-free grammar, we can convert it to Chomsky normal form while preserving its language.

children.title: Chomsky normal form
children.summary:
Here is a cohesive 5-sentence summary of the parent chapter 'Chomsky Normal Form':

The Chomsky Normal Form (CNF) transformation is a process used to convert context-free grammars into a standardized form, eliminating various types of rules that can complicate parsing and automaton construction. By applying CNF, grammars are simplified to have only two non-variable symbols on the right-hand side of each rule, making it easier to analyze and work with them. The transformation also introduces pushdown automata (PDAs), which are computational models that accept context-free languages in a way equivalent to CNF grammars. PDAs consist of a tape, stack, and state control system that work together to read input strings and determine acceptance or rejection. Ultimately, the conversion from CNF grammar to PDA provides an equivalent but more efficient way to analyze and process context-free languages, making it a fundamental tool in computer science and linguistics.

Pushdown automata introduction: Informal description of deterministic pushdown automaton consisting of tape, tape head, stack, stack head, and state control. Pushdown automata computation: Tape head reads symbol, stack top symbol, and current state; depends on input string, tape symbol, and stack symbol. Chomsky normal form conversion to pushdown automata equivalence: Context-free languages accepted by pushdown automata are exactly the same as those accepted by context-free grammars in Chomsky normal form.

children.title: Pushdown automata
children.summary:
Here is a dense 5-sentence routing summary of the section on pushdown automata:

Pushdown automata are computational models that accept context-free languages, defined as nondeterministic finite automata with a stack. A deterministic pushdown automaton consists of a tape alphabet Σ, a stack alphabet Γ, a set of states Q, and a transition function δ that specifies how the automaton moves and updates its state based on input symbols. The automaton accepts an input string if it terminates in a valid state with the blank symbol on the rightmost cell, and rejects otherwise. Deterministic pushdown automata can be constructed to accept specific languages, such as properly nested parentheses, by carefully designing the transition function δ. Nondeterministic pushdown automata extend deterministic models by allowing multiple computation steps for each input symbol, enabling them to recognize more complex context-free languages.

Another example, "Strings with 'b' in the middle", presents both deterministic and nondeterministic PDAs for accepting strings of {a, b}* with odd length and middle symbol 'b'. These examples showcase the construction and behavior of PDAs for specific languages, highlighting their acceptance properties and limitations. By examining these examples, readers can gain a deeper understanding of how PDAs are used to solve string recognition problems and explore the relationships between PDAs and other formal systems like context-free grammars.

children.title: Properly nested parentheses
children.summary:
Here is a dense, 5-sentence routing summary of this section on "Properly Nested Parentheses":

Constructing a deterministic pushdown automaton (PDA) for properly nested parentheses involves tracking the difference between the number of opening and closing brackets. The PDA reads input from left to right, pushing opening brackets onto the stack and popping them when encountering closing brackets. Acceptance occurs if the stack remains non-empty throughout processing and is empty at the end, indicating balanced nesting. A key transition function δ specifies actions for each symbol read, including pushing, popping, and terminating based on bracket counts. The PDA's behavior is defined by its states (e.g., q0 and q1), tape symbols (a and b), stack symbols ($ and S), and transition rules to ensure proper nesting.

children.summary:
Here is a dense 5-sentence routing summary:

This section describes deterministic pushdown automata (PDAs) for strings of the form "0n1n" and strings with "b" in the middle, accepting languages {0n1n : n ≥0} and {vbw : v ∈{a, b}∗, w ∈{a, b}∗, |v| = |w|}. The PDA for "0n1n" uses two states to track the number of 0s and 1s read, pushing S onto the stack when a 0 is read. For strings with "b" in the middle, the PDA uses two states to determine whether it has reached the middle symbol, using nondeterminism to guess when reading a b. The transition function δ specifies the actions taken by each state and tape symbol, including pushing S onto the stack or popping it from the stack. This section provides a detailed construction of these PDAs, demonstrating their acceptance properties for the specified languages.

children.title: Strings with b in the middle
children.summary:
Here is a dense, 5-sentence routing summary for this section:

"Strings with b in the middle": Constructing a nondeterministic pushdown automaton (PDA) to accept strings of {a, b}* having an odd length and whose middle symbol is b. The PDA uses two states: q (start state) and q' (state reached when middle symbol b is read). In q', the PDA pops symbols from the stack until it reaches a blank symbol 2, indicating the end of the input string. The PDA accepts strings if and only if it starts in q with an empty stack and reads a terminal symbol before reaching the middle symbol b. This problem demonstrates the equivalence between PDAs and context-free grammars, showing that nondeterministic PDAs are more powerful than deterministic PDAs.

children.title: Equivalence of pushdown automata and context-free grammars
children.summary:
Here is a dense, 5-sentence routing summary of the section "Equivalence of pushdown automata and context-free grammars":

Context-free languages can be equivalently represented by nondeterministic pushdown automata (PDAs) and context-free grammars (CFGs). The main result states that any CFG can be converted to a PDA, and vice versa. This conversion involves constructing a PDA from a CFG in Chomsky normal form, where the PDA's transition function is derived from the CFG's rules. The equivalence between PDAs and CFGs is proven by showing that a string is accepted by a PDA if and only if it can be derived from the start variable of a CFG. This result has significant implications for the study of context-free languages, including the pumping lemma for context-free languages, which states that any context-free language must have a finite pumping length.

children.title: The pumping lemma for context-free languages
children.summary:
Here is a cohesive 5-sentence summary of the parent chapter "The Pumping Lemma for Context-Free Languages":

The pumping lemma for context-free languages states that any context-free language must have a pumping length p, such that any string in the language with length greater than or equal to p can be divided into three substrings (uvx, vy, and yz) where |vy| ≥ 1, |vxy| ≤ p, and uvixyiz is also in the language for all i ≥ 0. This proof relies on Chomsky normal form and a result about parse trees, demonstrating that context-free languages have essential properties such as variable repetition and substring division. The pumping lemma has significant implications for the decidability of context-free languages, including the fact that some languages cannot be recognized by pushdown automata. Applications of the pumping lemma include proving that certain languages are not context-free, such as Language A = {anbncn : n ≥ 0} and Language B = {wwR : w ∈ \{a, b\}^*}. By demonstrating the existence of a pumping length p for any context-free language, the pumping lemma provides a powerful tool for proving non-context-freeness and advancing our understanding of the properties of context-free languages.

children.title: Proof of the pumping lemma
children.summary:
Here is a dense 5-sentence routing summary of the section "Proof of the Pumping Lemma" with core concepts and keywords to help a search engine find this page later:

The proof of the pumping lemma for context-free languages relies on Chomsky normal form and a result about parse trees, specifically that the length of a string is bounded by twice the height of its corresponding parse tree minus one. The pumping lemma states that any context-free language must have a pumping length p such that any string in the language with length greater than or equal to p can be divided into three substrings (uvx, vy, and yz) where |vy| ≥ 1, |vxy| ≤ p, and uvixyiz is also in the language for all i ≥ 0. The proof involves showing that there must exist a variable that occurs at least twice in a sequence of variables in a parse tree, leading to the conclusion that the string can be divided into three substrings as required by the pumping lemma. Additionally, the proof shows that |vy| ≥ 1 and |vxy| ≤ p, which are essential properties of context-free languages. The pumping lemma has significant implications for the decidability of context-free languages, including the fact that some languages cannot be recognized by a pushdown automaton.

children.title: Definition of a Turing machine
children.summary:
Here is a dense, 5-sentence routing summary of the section "Definition of a Turing machine":

Turing machines are simple models of real computers that can accept all context-free languages and solve problems on real computers. A deterministic Turing machine consists of an input alphabet Σ, tape alphabet Γ, finite set of states Q, start state q, accept state qaccept, reject state qreject, and transition function δ. The transition function δ determines the next state and symbol to write based on the current state and symbols read from the tapes. Computation terminates when the Turing machine enters either the accept or reject state, with acceptance indicating a valid input string and rejection indicating an invalid input string. A formal definition of a deterministic Turing machine is given by the 7-tuple M = (Σ, Γ, Q, δ, q, qaccept, qreject), where δ is the transition function that governs the machine's behavior.

children.title: The Church-Turing Thesis

Here is a dense, 5-sentence routing summary of this section:

The Church-Turing Thesis establishes an equivalence between various computational models, including Turing machines, one-tape Turing machines, k-tape Turing machines, non-deterministic Turing machines, Java programs, C++ programs, and Lisp programs. This thesis posits that every algorithm can be converted to a Turing machine, effectively defining an algorithm as a Turing machine. The concept of an algorithm has been debated since David Hilbert's 10th problem in 1900, which questions the existence of a finite process to determine whether a polynomial equation has integral roots. Matiyasevich proved in 1970 that such an algorithm does not exist, highlighting the importance of the Church-Turing Thesis as a rigorous definition of an algorithm. The thesis is now widely accepted by computer scientists, who define an algorithm as any computational process that can be converted to a Turing machine.

children.title: Decidable and Undecidable Languages

The study of decidability in languages reveals that some computational problems can be effectively solved using an algorithm, while others are undecidable. Decidability proofs rely on algorithmic conversions and simulations, leveraging established results from automata theory and formal language theory. The decidability of certain languages, such as ACFG, implies fundamental properties about context-free grammars and nondeterministic finite automata. In contrast, undecidable languages like ATM and Halt highlight the limitations of algorithmic decision-making for certain types of problems. Ultimately, the study of decidability provides insights into the complexity and limitations of algorithms in solving computational problems, shedding light on the inherent properties of computation and its boundaries.

children.title: Decidability

To prove decidability, an algorithm can be constructed to simulate the computation of M on input w and verify acceptance or rejection based on M's state transitions. This proof relies on the conversion of nondeterministic finite automata to equivalent deterministic finite automata, which is algorithmic in nature. The language ADFA is decidable due to the existence of such an algorithm, demonstrating that it can be effectively determined whether a given input string belongs to this language.

Similarly, the language ACFG (Accepting Context-Free Grammars) is also decidable, as demonstrated in Theorem 5.1.4, through the conversion of a context-free grammar to Chomsky normal form and subsequent simulation of derivations. To prove this, consider a string u encoding a context-free grammar G and a string w ∈Σ∗, and decide whether or not w ∈L(G) by converting G to Chomsky normal form and checking all possible derivations of length 2n-1. The language ACFG is decidable because it can be decided using an algorithm that checks all possible derivations in the equivalent grammar G'. Every context-free language is decidable, as shown by the proof that ACFG is decidable and any context-free language L(G) has a corresponding Chomsky normal form grammar.

children.title: The language ATM
children.summary:
Here is a dense 5-sentence routing summary of the section "The language ATM":

ATM (Accepting Turing Machines) is undecidable, meaning there cannot exist an algorithm to determine whether a given Turing machine accepts a specific input string in finite time. The proof involves contradiction, assuming ATM is decidable and constructing a new Turing machine D that can determine the behavior of any Turing machine M on its own description as input. This leads to a paradox when considering the behavior of D on itself, resulting in a contradiction and proving ATM is undecidable. A similar argument applies to the language Halt (Halting Problem), which asks whether a given Java program terminates on a specific input string. Both ATM and Halt are undecidable, highlighting the limitations of algorithmic decision-making for certain types of problems.

children.title: The Halting Problem
children.summary:
Here is a dense, 5-sentence routing summary of the section "The Halting Problem":

The Halting Problem is undecidable, meaning there cannot exist a Java program H that can determine whether any given Java program P will terminate on a given input w. The proof by contradiction assumes H exists and leads to a self-referential paradox when running Q(⟨Q⟩), where Q is another algorithm derived from H. This paradox arises due to the existence of a bijection between two sets, demonstrating that the language Halt has the same size as the set of natural numbers N. The undecidability of Halt implies that no algorithm can determine whether any program will halt on a given input, highlighting the limitations of computation. The Halting Problem is a fundamental result in computability theory, demonstrating the inherent complexity and limitations of algorithms in solving certain problems.

children.title: Rice's Theorem
children.summary:
Here is a cohesive 5-sentence summary of Rice's Theorem:

Rice's Theorem states that if a language P is decidable, then the language Halt (containing all Turing machines that halt on any input) must also be decidable. To prove this, a new Turing machine TMw is constructed to simulate the behavior of any given Turing machine M on an arbitrary input w, leading to a contradiction with Theorem 5.1.7. This contradiction implies that P cannot actually be decidable, and by extension, no language can be both decidable and undecidable. By repeating the proof with P replaced by its complement, it is shown that Halt must indeed be decidable if P is decidable, thus proving Rice's Theorem. Ultimately, this theorem establishes a fundamental relationship between decidability and computability in the context of Turing machines.

children.title: Proof of Rice's Theorem
children.summary:
Here is a dense, 5-sentence routing summary for the section "Proof of Rice's Theorem":

Rice's Theorem states that if a language P is decidable, then the language Halt (containing all Turing machines that halt on any input) is also decidable. To prove this, we construct a new Turing machine TMw that takes as input a string ⟨M, w⟩ and runs M on w; if M halts, it runs another Turing machine M2 on x; if M2 accepts, the resulting machine terminates in accept state else rejects. We show that L(TMw) = L(M2), which implies that Halt is decidable since P is assumed to be decidable. This leads to a contradiction with Theorem 5.1.7, implying that P must actually be undecidable. Therefore, we repeat the proof with P replaced by its complement, showing that no language can be both decidable and undecidable, thus proving Rice's Theorem.

By proving that Hilbert's problem and languages like ATM are enumerable, we gain insight into their decidability and demonstrate the power of systematic searching in solving complex problems.

Here is a dense 5-sentence routing summary of the section "Where does the term 'enumerable' come from?" with core concepts and keywords:

The term "enumerable" originates from Deﬁnition 5.4.1, which defines a language as enumerable if there exists an enumerator Turing machine E that writes all strings in the language to the printer at least once, but never sends a string not in the language to the printer. An enumerator is a Turing machine with a print tape and print state that simulates computation on input w, ensuring every string in the language is sent to the printer. Theorem 5.4.4 proves that the language ATM is enumerable by constructing an enumerator Turing machine M that takes an arbitrary string as input and terminates in the accept state if the input belongs to the language. Conversely, Theorem 5.5.2 shows that a language is enumerable if and only if it has an enumerator. The set of enumerable languages is countable, as demonstrated in Section 5.6.1 by mapping each enumerable language to a unique binary string encoding its corresponding Turing machine.

The distinction between countability and enumerability highlights a fundamental aspect of formal language theory, with enumerable languages being a subset of all possible languages. Decidable languages, which are a subset of enumerable languages, provide an important counterexample to the assumption that every language can be enumerated. Ultimately, this understanding underscores that most languages are not enumerable, with only enumerable languages being countable.

The study of complexity theory examines the efficiency and limitations of algorithms in solving computational problems, focusing on the concept of running time as a classification criterion. The complexity class P consists of languages that can be decided in polynomial time, while NP includes languages verifiable in polynomial time, with P being a proper subset of NP. Understanding the relationships between these classes, including the containment of P within NP and the open problem of whether every language in NP can also be solved in polynomial time (P vs. NP), is crucial for advancing our knowledge of computational complexity theory. The concept of NP-completeness provides a framework for comparing the difficulty of different languages in NP, with examples such as SAT, 3SAT, Clique, and Traveling Salesperson Problem demonstrating the complexity of various problems. Ultimately, resolving the P vs. NP problem and understanding the intricacies of complexity classes like P, NP, and EXP is essential for developing more efficient algorithms and advancing computer science.

**Routing Summary:**

The concept of polynomial time is introduced, which refers to an algorithm's running time being bounded by O(n^k), where k ≥ 1. The complexity classes P and FP are defined, where P consists of decidable languages that can be decided in polynomial time, and FP consists of computable functions that can be computed in polynomial time. Examples of languages and functions in the classes P and FP include palindromes, addition, multiplication, and other basic arithmetic operations.

children.title: The complexity class P
children.summary:
Here is a cohesive 5-sentence summary for the parent chapter "The complexity class P":

The complexity class P consists of languages that can be decided in polynomial time, meaning their membership or non-membership can be determined using an algorithm that runs in time proportional to the size of the input. This class includes decidable languages such as palindromes and context-free languages, as well as computable functions like F1, F2, and F3. The complexity of these problems is demonstrated through algorithms with a polynomial time complexity, showcasing that they can be solved efficiently. In contrast to NP, which involves verifying membership in a language rather than deciding it, P focuses on decidability. By understanding the properties of languages in P, researchers can develop more efficient algorithms for solving important computational problems.

children.title: Some examples

The complexity class P is defined as the set of languages that can be decided in polynomial time, and FP is defined as the set of functions that can be computed in polynomial time. Examples include palindromes (decidable), some functions such as F1, F2, and F3 (computable), context-free languages (decidable), and 2-colorable graphs (also decidable). The algorithm for deciding 2-colorability runs in O(n) time, where n is the number of vertices in the graph. This demonstrates that 2Color ∈P. Additionally, the complexity class NP is introduced as the set of languages that can be verified in polynomial time, with examples including k-colorable graphs (verifiable).

children.title: The complexity class NP
children.summary:
Here is a cohesive 5-sentence summary for the parent chapter 'The complexity class NP':

The complexity class NP, which includes languages verifiable in polynomial time, has been shown to contain P through a formal proof establishing that P ⊆NP. This relationship highlights the complexity of verifying solutions for NP-languages, as opposed to computing them directly, and demonstrates that every language in NP can be decided in exponential time. The containment of P in NP is further underscored by the fact that NP is a subset of EXP, where EXP includes languages that can be decided in exponential time using a polynomial number of possible solutions. Despite this relationship, the exact classification of P within NP remains an open problem with significant implications for computer science. Ultimately, understanding the intricate relationships between P, NP, and EXP is crucial for advancing our knowledge of computational complexity theory and its applications.

children.title: P is contained in NP
children.summary:
Here is a dense, 5-sentence routing summary of this section:

"P is contained in NP" is proven through a formal proof (Theorem 6.3.7) that establishes P ⊆NP, where P contains languages solvable in polynomial time and NP contains languages verifiable in polynomial time. The proof relies on the definition of NP, which states that for any language A ∈NP, there exists a polynomial p and a language B ∈P such that w ∈A if and only if there exists an s with |s| ≤p(|w|) and ⟨w, s⟩∈B. Deciding whether a given string belongs to an NP-language requires finding a string s satisfying the above condition, which can be done in exponential time. This highlights the complexity of verifying solutions for NP-languages, as opposed to computing them directly. The proof demonstrates that P ⊆NP, providing insight into the relationship between solvability and verifiability in computational complexity theory.

children.title: Deciding NP-languages in exponential time
children.summary:
Here is a dense, 5-sentence routing summary of the section "Deciding NP-languages in exponential time":

NP ⊆ EXP, where EXP is the class of languages that can be decided in exponential time, as every language in NP can be decided in exponential time using a polynomial number of possible solutions. The complexity class P is contained in NP, and it is not known whether P is a proper subclass of NP or whether P = NP. A non-deterministic algorithm decides a language in NP by exploring all possible solutions in exponential time, whereas deterministic algorithms decide languages in P in polynomial time. The running time of the algorithm that decides SOS, a language in NP, is at least exponential in the length of the input, demonstrating that every language in NP can be decided in exponential time. This result implies that P ⊆ EXP and highlights the importance of understanding the relationship between P, NP, and EXP in computer science.

This section discusses the complexities of Non-Deterministic Polynomial time (NP) and its relationship with other classes, including P and EXP. The open problem of whether P is a proper subclass of NP or equal to it has significant implications for computer science. NP is known to be a subset of EXP, but the exact relationship between these two classes remains unknown. Non-deterministic algorithms, which consider multiple possibilities at each step, are distinct from deterministic algorithms and can solve certain problems more efficiently. The language SOS is used as an example of a problem that can be solved by a non-deterministic algorithm in polynomial time.

children.title: Non-deterministic algorithms
children.summary:
Here is a dense, 5-sentence routing summary of the section on non-deterministic algorithms:

"Non-deterministic algorithms are computational models where multiple possibilities for the next computation step exist, allowing for parallel exploration of different solutions. The class NP (Non-deterministic Polynomial time) comprises languages that can be decided by such algorithms in polynomial time, with P being a proper subset of NP. The Clay Mathematics Institute offers a $1 million prize for solving the P vs. NP problem, which seeks to determine whether every language in NP can also be solved in polynomial time. NP-complete languages are those that are at least as hard as the hardest problems in NP, meaning they cannot be solved in polynomial time even if all other NP problems could be solved efficiently. The concept of NP-completeness provides a framework for comparing the difficulty of different languages in NP."

children.title: NP-complete languages
children.summary:
Here is a cohesive 5-sentence summary of the parent chapter "NP-complete languages":

The concept of NP-completeness is defined as a language that belongs to the most difficult classes in NP, with two conditions: being in NP and every language in NP reducible to it in polynomial time. An NP-complete language implies P = NP if it can be solved exactly in polynomial time, and conversely, if P = NP then all NP languages are solvable in polynomial time. The reduction SOS ≤P KS establishes that the sum of subsets problem is equivalent to the knapsack problem, while Clique ≤P 3SAT shows a connection between the clique problem and Boolean formula satisfiability. Examples of NP-complete languages include SAT (Satisfiability), 3SAT, Clique, Traveling Salesperson Problem, Bin Packing, Time Tables, Motion Planning, and Map Labeling, which demonstrate the complexity of various problems in computer science. Ultimately, proving that a language is NP-complete provides fundamental insights into the limitations of algorithms for solving these problems efficiently and highlights the importance of understanding the relationship between polynomial-time computability and NP-completeness.

children.title: Two examples of reductions
children.summary:
Here is a dense 5-sentence routing summary of the section "Two examples of reductions":

The reduction SOS ≤P KS establishes that the sum of subsets problem can be reduced to the knapsack problem, demonstrating that both problems are in P. The reduction Clique ≤P 3SAT shows that the clique problem can be reduced to the Boolean formula satisfiability problem, highlighting the connection between two seemingly unrelated problems in NP. These reductions demonstrate the concept of polynomial-time reducibility and its implications for the complexity hierarchy. By establishing these reductions, we can conclude that both SOS and Clique are NP-complete languages, as they belong to the most difficult classes in NP. Ultimately, Theorem 6.5.10 states that an NP-complete language is in P if and only if P = NP, providing a fundamental result on the relationship between polynomial-time computability and NP-completeness.

children.title: Definition of NP-completeness
children.summary:
Here is a dense 5-sentence routing summary of the section on "Definition of NP-completeness":

NP-completeness is defined as a language B that belongs to the most difficult languages in NP, with two conditions: (1) B is in NP and (2) every language A in NP can be reduced to B in polynomial time. An NP-complete language B implies P = NP if it can be solved exactly in polynomial time, and conversely, if P = NP then all NP languages are solvable in polynomial time. Theorem 6.5.10 states that an NP-complete language B is equivalent to P = NP, meaning that if B is in P, then P = NP, and vice versa. To prove the NP-completeness of a language C, one can reduce an already known NP-complete language B to C using polynomial-time reductions, as stated in Theorem 6.5.11.

children.title: An NP-complete domino game
children.summary:
## Step 1: Understand the problem
We are given a language SAT, which consists of Boolean formulas ϕ in the variables x1, x2, ..., xm having the form ϕ = C1 ∧C2 ∧... ∧Ck, where each Ci is of the form Ci = ℓi1 ∨ℓi2 ∨... ∨ℓikj. We need to prove that SAT is NP-complete.

## Step 2: Show that SAT ∈NP
It is clear that SAT ∈NP, since we can verify in polynomial time whether a given formula ϕ is satisﬁable by checking all possible assignments of truth-values to the variables x1, x2, ..., xm.

## Step 3: Find a function f ∈FP that maps input strings for Domino to input strings for SAT
We need to find a function f ∈FP that maps an arbitrary domino game D to a formula ϕ in SAT. We will use this function to show that Domino ≤P SAT.

## Step 4: Construct the formula encoded by the string f(⟨D⟩)
Let us consider an arbitrary domino game D. Let k be the number of tile types, and let the frame have t rows and t columns. We denote the tile types by T1, T2, ..., Tk. For each tile type Ti in D, we construct a variable xi in SAT. We then construct a formula ϕ in SAT as follows:

ϕ = (x1 ∧ x2 ∧ ... ∧ xk) ∧ (∀i ∈ {1, 2, ..., k}) (Ti = Tj ∨ ¬Ti)

where Ti is the tile type corresponding to the variable xi.

## Step 5: Show that domino game D is solvable if and only if the formula encoded by the string f(⟨D⟩) is satisﬁable
We need to show that domino game D is solvable if and only if the formula ϕ in SAT encoded by the string f(⟨D⟩)) is satisﬁable.

If domino game D is solvable, then there exists a solution for the domino game. This means that we can assign truth-values to the variables x1, x2, ..., xm such that the entire formula ϕ is true. Therefore, the formula ϕ in SAT encoded by the string f(⟨D⟩)) is satisﬁable.

Conversely, if the formula ϕ in SAT encoded by the string f(⟨D⟩)) is satisﬁable, then there exists a truth-value assignment for the variables x1, x2, ..., xm such that the entire formula ϕ is true. This means that we can assign truth-values to the variables xi such that Ti = Tj ∨ ¬Ti for all i ∈ {1, 2, ..., k}. Therefore, domino game D is solvable.

## Step 6: Conclude that SAT is NP-complete
Since we have shown that Domino ≤P SAT and SAT ∈NP, it follows from Theorem 6.5.11 that SAT is NP-complete.

The final answer is: $\boxed{1}$

The language SAT (Satisfiability) is proven to be NP-complete by mapping domino games to Boolean formulas and showing that the satisfiability of these formulas corresponds to the solvability of the domino game. The language 3SAT is also shown to be NP-complete, with a reduction from SAT to 3SAT. children.title: Summary
children.summary:
Here is a dense, 5-sentence routing summary for this section:

Regular languages are closed under union, concatenation, and star operations, but not necessarily intersection or complement. Context-free languages are equivalent to regular languages and can be converted to nondeterministic pushdown automata, while some context-free languages (e.g., {anbn : n ≥0}) are not regular. The pumping lemma for regular and context-free languages provides a property that every language possesses, which is used to prove non-regularity of certain languages. Decidable and enumerable languages are defined based on computational devices like Turing machines and Java programs, with some languages (e.g., {anbncn : n ≥0}) being decidable but not context-free or enumerable. The class P consists of languages decidible in polynomial time by deterministic Turing machines, while NP consists of languages decidable in polynomial time by nondeterministic Turing machines, with some languages (e.g., the Halting Problem) being enumerable but not decidable.

Keywords: regular languages, context-free languages, pumping lemma, decidable and enumerable languages, computational complexity classes P and NP.
